content.text: Testing is not an afterthought in a FastAPI application — it is the mechanism by which the dependency injection system, response model contracts, and async behavior are verified to work together correctly under realistic conditions.

Testing FastAPI applications encompasses verifying the behavior of HTTP endpoints, dependency injection chains, database interactions, authentication flows, middleware, background tasks, and WebSocket connections. FastAPI provides first-class testing support through its integration with Starlette's
with ASGI transport (asynchronous), both of which exercise the full application stack — routing, middleware, validation, serialization — without running an actual HTTP server.
for HTTP client simulation, and FastAPI's
mechanism for replacing production dependencies (database sessions, auth, external services) with test-controlled alternatives.
FastAPI's design — type annotations, Pydantic validation, dependency injection, response models — creates implicit contracts throughout the application. A response model that strips

only works correctly if it is actually applied. An auth dependency that validates JWTs only protects an endpoint if it is actually wired up. A background task that opens its own database session only works correctly if the session factory is correctly configured.
Tests are the mechanism that verifies these contracts actually hold. Without them:
Response model filtering can be accidentally bypassed
Authentication dependencies can be silently detached during refactoring
Pydantic validation behavior changes across versions without notice
Database session lifecycle bugs only appear in production under load
Async edge cases — cancelled requests, concurrent session access — are invisible until production incidents

pip install pytest pytest-asyncio httpx
# Optional but recommended
pip install pytest-cov factory-boy faker pytest-xdist
Async HTTP client for testing ASGI apps
Sync HTTP client wrapping the ASGI app
Replace production dependencies with test versions
Model instance factories for test data

# pyproject.toml
[tool.pytest.ini_options]
asyncio_mode = "auto"             # All async tests run without @pytest.mark.asyncio
testpaths = ["tests"]
python_files = ["test_*.py"]
python_classes = ["Test*"]
python_functions = ["test_*"]
addopts = [
    "--strict-markers",
    "--tb=short",
    "-v",
]
markers = [
    "unit: Unit tests with no external dependencies",
    "integration: Tests requiring database or external services",
    "slow: Tests that take more than 1 second",
]

[tool.coverage.run]
source = ["app"]
omit = ["tests/*", "alembic/*"]

[tool.coverage.report]
fail_under = 80
show_missing = true
function is automatically treated as an async test — no per-test decorator needed.

tests/
├── conftest.py              # Session/module fixtures, DB setup, app factory
├── factories.py             # factory_boy model factories
├── test_health.py           # Smoke tests
├── unit/
│   ├── test_schemas.py      # Pydantic schema validation
│   ├── test_services.py     # Business logic (mocked repositories)
│   └── test_utils.py        # Pure utility functions
├── integration/
│   ├── test_auth.py         # Auth flow end-to-end
│   ├── test_users.py        # User CRUD endpoints
│   ├── test_orders.py       # Order endpoints with DB
│   └── test_websockets.py   # WebSocket behavior
└── conftest.py
The standard approach for async FastAPI testing uses
, which calls the ASGI application directly without opening a network socket:

# tests/conftest.py
import pytest
from httpx import AsyncClient, ASGITransport
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker, AsyncSession

from main import create_application
from database import get_db
from config import Settings

TEST_DATABASE_URL = "postgresql+asyncpg://user:password@localhost:5432/test_db"

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def test_settings():
    return Settings(
        database_url=TEST_DATABASE_URL,
        secret_key="test-secret-key-exactly-32-chars!!",
        environment="testing",
        debug=True,
    )

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def app(test_settings):
    return create_application(settings=test_settings)

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
async def test_engine(test_settings):
    engine = create_async_engine(test_settings.async_database_url, echo=False)
    yield engine
    await engine.dispose()

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
async def create_tables(test_engine):
    """Create all tables once per test session."""
    from database import Base
    async with test_engine.begin() as conn:
        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all)
    yield
    async with test_engine.begin() as conn:
        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.drop_all)

@pytest.fixture
async def db_session(test_engine, create_tables):
    """
    Each test runs in a transaction that is rolled back afterward.
    This resets the database state between tests without DROP/CREATE overhead.
    """
    async with test_engine.connect() as conn:
        await conn.begin()
        session = AsyncSession(bind=conn, expire_on_commit=False)
        yield session
        await session.close()
        await conn.rollback()

@pytest.fixture
async def client(app, db_session):
    """HTTP test client with database dependency overridden."""
    async def override_get_db():
        yield db_session

    app.dependency_overrides[get_db] = override_get_db

    async with AsyncClient(
        transport=ASGITransport(app=app),
        base_url="http://test"
    ) as ac:
        yield ac

    app.dependency_overrides.clear()

The transaction rollback pattern is the key optimization — instead of truncating or recreating tables between tests, each test runs inside a transaction that is rolled back when the test ends. This is significantly faster than schema recreation and guarantees complete isolation between tests.

# tests/integration/test_users.py
import pytest
from httpx import AsyncClient

async def test_create_user(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
        "username": "alice",
        "email": "alice@example.com",
        "password": "securepassword123"
    })
    assert response.status_code == 201
    data = response.json()
    assert data["username"] == "alice"
    assert data["email"] == "alice@example.com"
    assert "id" in data
    assert "hashed_password" not in data   # Verify response_model strips sensitive fields

async def test_create_user_duplicate_email(client: AsyncClient):
    payload = {
        "username": "bob",
        "email": "bob@example.com",
        "password": "password123"
    }
    # First creation succeeds
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json=payload)
    assert response.status_code == 201

    # Second creation with same email fails
    payload["username"] = "bob2"  # Different username, same email
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json=payload)
    assert response.status_code == 409
    assert "email" in response.json()["detail"].lower()

async def test_get_user_not_found(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    response = await client.get("/api/v1/users/99999", headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 404

async def test_get_user(client: AsyncClient, existing_user, auth_headers: dict):
    response = await client.get(
        f"/api/v1/users/{existing_user.id}",
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    assert data["id"] == existing_user.id
    assert data["username"] == existing_user.username

async def test_list_users_pagination(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict, db_session):
    """Verify pagination headers and response structure."""
    # Create 25 users
    from tests.factories import UserFactory
    for _ in range(25):
        await UserFactory.create_async(session=db_session)

    response = await client.get(
        "/api/v1/users/?skip=0&limit=10",
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    assert len(data["items"]) == 10
    assert data["total"] >= 25
    assert data["has_next"] is True
    assert data["has_prev"] is False
FastAPI's automatic validation produces structured 422 responses. Testing these explicitly verifies that validation rules are correctly configured:

async def test_create_user_invalid_email(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
        "username": "alice",
        "email": "not-an-email",
        "password": "password123"
    })
    assert response.status_code == 422
    errors = response.json()["detail"]
    assert any(e["loc"] == ["body", "email"] for e in errors)

async def test_create_user_short_password(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
        "username": "alice",
        "email": "alice@example.com",
        "password": "short"   # Fails min_length=8
    })
    assert response.status_code == 422
    errors = response.json()["detail"]
    field_names = [".".join(str(l) for l in e["loc"]) for e in errors]
    assert "body.password" in field_names

async def test_create_user_missing_required_field(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
        "username": "alice"
        # email and password missing
    })
    assert response.status_code == 422
    error_locs = {".".join(str(l) for l in e["loc"]) for e in response.json()["detail"]}
    assert "body.email" in error_locs
    assert "body.password" in error_locs

@pytest.mark.parametrize("username,expected_status", [
    ("alice", 201),            # Valid
    ("a", 422),                # Too short (min_length=3)
    ("a" * 51, 422),           # Too long (max_length=50)
    ("alice!", 422),           # Invalid character (pattern)
    ("alice_123", 201),        # Valid with underscore
    ("ALICE", 201),            # Valid uppercase
])
async def test_username_validation(client: AsyncClient, username: str, expected_status: int):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
        "username": username,
        "email": f"{username.lower().replace('!','')}@example.com",
        "password": "validpassword123"
    })
    assert response.status_code == expected_status

# tests/conftest.py (additions)
from jose import jwt
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

@pytest.fixture
async def existing_user(db_session):
    """Create a real user in the test database."""
    from passlib.context import CryptContext
    from domains.users.models import User

    pwd_context = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto")
    user = User(
        username="testuser",
        email="testuser@example.com",
        hashed_password=pwd_context.hash("testpassword123"),
        is_active=True
    )
    db_session.add(user)
    await db_session.flush()
    return user

@pytest.fixture
async def admin_user(db_session):
    from domains.users.models import User
    from passlib.context import CryptContext

    pwd_context = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto")
    user = User(
        username="adminuser",
        email="admin@example.com",
        hashed_password=pwd_context.hash("adminpassword123"),
        is_active=True,
        roles=["admin"]
    )
    db_session.add(user)
    await db_session.flush()
    return user

@pytest.fixture
def auth_headers(existing_user, test_settings):
    """Generate a valid JWT for the test user."""
    token = jwt.encode(
        {
            "sub": str(existing_user.id),
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(hours=1)
        },
        test_settings.secret_key,
        algorithm=test_settings.algorithm
    )
    return {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

@pytest.fixture
def admin_headers(admin_user, test_settings):
    token = jwt.encode(
        {
            "sub": str(admin_user.id),
            "roles": ["admin"],
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(hours=1)
        },
        test_settings.secret_key,
        algorithm=test_settings.algorithm
    )
    return {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

# tests/integration/test_auth.py
async def test_login_success(client: AsyncClient, existing_user):
    response = await client.post("/auth/token", data={
        "username": "testuser@example.com",
        "password": "testpassword123"
    })
    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    assert "access_token" in data
    assert data["token_type"] == "bearer"

    # Verify token is actually valid
    from jose import jwt
    payload = jwt.decode(data["access_token"], "test-secret-key-exactly-32-chars!!", algorithms=["HS256"])
    assert payload["sub"] == str(existing_user.id)

async def test_login_wrong_password(client: AsyncClient, existing_user):
    response = await client.post("/auth/token", data={
        "username": "testuser@example.com",
        "password": "wrongpassword"
    })
    assert response.status_code == 401
    # Verify the error doesn't reveal which field was wrong
    assert "incorrect" in response.json()["detail"].lower()

async def test_login_nonexistent_user(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.post("/auth/token", data={
        "username": "nobody@example.com",
        "password": "password123"
    })
    assert response.status_code == 401

async def test_protected_endpoint_without_token(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.get("/api/v1/users/me")
    assert response.status_code == 401

async def test_protected_endpoint_with_invalid_token(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.get(
        "/api/v1/users/me",
        headers={"Authorization": "Bearer invalid.token.here"}
    )
    assert response.status_code == 401

async def test_protected_endpoint_with_expired_token(client: AsyncClient, existing_user, test_settings):
    expired_token = jwt.encode(
        {
            "sub": str(existing_user.id),
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=1)  # Already expired
        },
        test_settings.secret_key,
        algorithm=test_settings.algorithm
    )
    response = await client.get(
        "/api/v1/users/me",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {expired_token}"}
    )
    assert response.status_code == 401

async def test_admin_only_endpoint_as_regular_user(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers):
    response = await client.delete("/api/v1/users/1", headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 403

async def test_admin_only_endpoint_as_admin(client: AsyncClient, admin_headers, existing_user):
    response = await client.delete(
        f"/api/v1/users/{existing_user.id}",
        headers=admin_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 204
is FastAPI's mechanism for replacing any
dependency with a test alternative. This enables testing without mocking at the import level.
For unit-style endpoint tests where the focus is on business logic, not auth:

# tests/conftest.py
from domains.users.models import User
from core.security import get_current_user

@pytest.fixture
def mock_current_user():
    return User(
        id=1,
        username="mockuser",
        email="mock@example.com",
        is_active=True,
        roles=[]
    )

@pytest.fixture
async def authenticated_client(app, db_session, mock_current_user):
    """Client where auth is bypassed — user is always mock_current_user."""
    async def override_get_db():
        yield db_session

    async def override_get_current_user():
        return mock_current_user

    app.dependency_overrides[get_db] = override_get_db
    app.dependency_overrides[get_current_user] = override_get_current_user

    async with AsyncClient(
        transport=ASGITransport(app=app),
        base_url="http://test"
    ) as ac:
        yield ac

    app.dependency_overrides.clear()

from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock

@pytest.fixture
async def client_with_mock_email(app, db_session):
    """Replace email service with a mock that captures sent emails."""
    sent_emails = []

    async def mock_send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str):
        sent_emails.append({"to": to, "subject": subject, "body": body})

    async def override_get_db():
        yield db_session

    app.dependency_overrides[get_db] = override_get_db
    app.dependency_overrides[get_email_service] = lambda: mock_send_email

    async with AsyncClient(
        transport=ASGITransport(app=app),
        base_url="http://test"
    ) as ac:
        yield ac, sent_emails

    app.dependency_overrides.clear()

async def test_registration_sends_welcome_email(client_with_mock_email):
    client, sent_emails = client_with_mock_email

    response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
        "username": "newuser",
        "email": "newuser@example.com",
        "password": "password123"
    })
    assert response.status_code == 201
    assert len(sent_emails) == 1
    assert sent_emails[0]["to"] == "newuser@example.com"
    assert "welcome" in sent_emails[0]["subject"].lower()
Service classes contain business logic and should be tested independently of the HTTP layer:

# tests/unit/test_user_service.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock, patch
from fastapi import HTTPException

from domains.users.service import UserService
from domains.users.schemas import UserCreate, UserUpdate
from domains.users.models import User

@pytest.fixture
def mock_repository():
    repo = AsyncMock()
    repo.get_by_id = AsyncMock(return_value=None)
    repo.get_by_email = AsyncMock(return_value=None)
    repo.create = AsyncMock()
    repo.update = AsyncMock()
    return repo

@pytest.fixture
def user_service(mock_repository):
    return UserService(repository=mock_repository)

@pytest.fixture
def sample_user():
    return User(
        id=1,
        username="alice",
        email="alice@example.com",
        hashed_password="$2b$12$hashed...",
        is_active=True
    )

async def test_create_user_success(user_service, mock_repository, sample_user):
    mock_repository.get_by_email.return_value = None   # Email not taken
    mock_repository.create.return_value = sample_user

    result = await user_service.create_user(UserCreate(
        username="alice",
        email="alice@example.com",
        password="securepassword"
    ))

    assert result == sample_user
    mock_repository.create.assert_awaited_once()
    # Verify password was hashed, not stored plaintext
    call_kwargs = mock_repository.create.call_args.kwargs
    assert call_kwargs.get("hashed_password") != "securepassword"
    assert call_kwargs.get("hashed_password", "").startswith("$2b$")

async def test_create_user_email_conflict(user_service, mock_repository, sample_user):
    mock_repository.get_by_email.return_value = sample_user  # Email already taken

    with pytest.raises(HTTPException) as exc_info:
        await user_service.create_user(UserCreate(
            username="alice2",
            email="alice@example.com",
            password="password123"
        ))

    assert exc_info.value.status_code == 409

async def test_update_user_email_uniqueness(user_service, mock_repository, sample_user):
    existing_user_with_email = User(id=2, email="taken@example.com", username="other")
    mock_repository.get_by_id.return_value = sample_user
    mock_repository.get_by_email.return_value = existing_user_with_email

    with pytest.raises(HTTPException) as exc_info:
        await user_service.update_user(1, UserUpdate(email="taken@example.com"))

    assert exc_info.value.status_code == 409

async def test_deactivate_nonexistent_user(user_service, mock_repository):
    mock_repository.get_by_id.return_value = None

    result = await user_service.deactivate_user(99999)
    assert result is False

async def test_verify_password(user_service):
    from passlib.context import CryptContext
    ctx = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto")
    hashed = ctx.hash("correct-password")

    assert user_service.verify_password("correct-password", hashed) is True
    assert user_service.verify_password("wrong-password", hashed) is False

Background tasks execute after the response is returned, making them invisible to a basic response assertion. The approach is to either await them directly or use dependency overrides to capture their execution:

# tests/integration/test_background_tasks.py
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch

async def test_registration_queues_welcome_email(client: AsyncClient, db_session):
    """
    Verify the background task is called with correct arguments.
    Using patch to intercept the task before it executes.
    """
    with patch("domains.users.router.send_welcome_email") as mock_email:
        mock_email.return_value = None

        response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
            "username": "newuser",
            "email": "newuser@example.com",
            "password": "password123"
        })

        assert response.status_code == 201
        # Verify the task was scheduled with correct args
        mock_email.assert_called_once_with(
            email="newuser@example.com",
            username="newuser"
        )

async def test_background_task_failure_does_not_affect_response(client: AsyncClient):
    """Verify that a failing background task doesn't change the HTTP response."""
    with patch("domains.users.router.send_welcome_email", side_effect=Exception("SMTP failed")):
        response = await client.post("/api/v1/users/", json={
            "username": "alice",
            "email": "alice@example.com",
            "password": "password123"
        })
        # Response should still be 201 — background task failure is invisible to caller
        assert response.status_code == 201

# tests/integration/test_websockets.py
import pytest
from starlette.testclient import TestClient   # Sync client for WebSocket testing

from main import app

def test_websocket_echo():
    """Basic WebSocket connectivity and message echo."""
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        with client.websocket_connect("/ws/echo") as ws:
            ws.send_text("hello")
            data = ws.receive_text()
            assert data == "Echo: hello"

def test_websocket_json_protocol():
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        with client.websocket_connect("/ws/feed") as ws:
            ws.send_json({"type": "ping"})
            response = ws.receive_json()
            assert response["type"] == "pong"

def test_websocket_auth_rejected_without_token():
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        with pytest.raises(Exception):   # WebSocket close with 1008
            with client.websocket_connect("/ws/protected") as ws:
                ws.receive_text()   # Should fail — connection rejected

def test_websocket_auth_accepted_with_valid_token(existing_user, test_settings):
    from jose import jwt
    from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

    token = jwt.encode(
        {"sub": str(existing_user.id), "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(hours=1)},
        test_settings.secret_key,
        algorithm=test_settings.algorithm
    )
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        with client.websocket_connect(f"/ws/protected?token={token}") as ws:
            ws.send_text("hello")
            data = ws.receive_text()
            assert data is not None

def test_websocket_broadcast(db_session):
    """Verify messages from one client reach other connected clients."""
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        with client.websocket_connect("/ws/chat/room1?username=alice") as ws1:
            with client.websocket_connect("/ws/chat/room1?username=bob") as ws2:
                # Alice sends a message
                ws1.send_text("Hello from Alice")

                # Bob should receive it
                received = ws2.receive_text()
                data = json.loads(received)
                assert data["user"] == "alice"
                assert data["text"] == "Hello from Alice"

# tests/integration/test_uploads.py
import io
from pathlib import Path

async def test_upload_valid_image(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    # Create a minimal valid JPEG (magic bytes only)
    jpeg_content = b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0' + b'\x00' * 100

    response = await client.post(
        "/api/v1/uploads/avatar/",
        files={"file": ("avatar.jpg", io.BytesIO(jpeg_content), "image/jpeg")},
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 201
    data = response.json()
    assert "storage_key" in data
    assert data["content_type"] == "image/jpeg"

async def test_upload_invalid_content_type(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    """Reject files whose content doesn't match declared type."""
    # Send executable content with JPEG content type
    exe_content = b'MZ\x90\x00'  # PE executable magic bytes

    response = await client.post(
        "/api/v1/uploads/avatar/",
        files={"file": ("malicious.jpg", io.BytesIO(exe_content), "image/jpeg")},
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 400

async def test_upload_file_too_large(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    large_content = b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0' + b'\x00' * (11 * 1024 * 1024)  # 11MB

    response = await client.post(
        "/api/v1/uploads/document/",
        files={"file": ("large.jpg", io.BytesIO(large_content), "image/jpeg")},
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 413

async def test_upload_path_traversal_filename(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    jpeg_content = b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0' + b'\x00' * 100

    response = await client.post(
        "/api/v1/uploads/avatar/",
        files={"file": ("../../etc/passwd", io.BytesIO(jpeg_content), "image/jpeg")},
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    # Should succeed but store with sanitized filename
    assert response.status_code == 201
    data = response.json()
    assert ".." not in data.get("filename", "")
    assert "/" not in data.get("filename", "")

# tests/test_middleware.py
async def test_request_id_header_returned(client: AsyncClient):
    """Verify RequestIDMiddleware adds X-Request-ID to every response."""
    response = await client.get("/health")
    assert "x-request-id" in response.headers

async def test_provided_request_id_is_echoed(client: AsyncClient):
    """Verify client-provided request ID is preserved."""
    custom_id = "my-trace-id-12345"
    response = await client.get("/health", headers={"X-Request-ID": custom_id})
    assert response.headers["x-request-id"] == custom_id

async def test_cors_allowed_origin(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.get(
        "/health",
        headers={"Origin": "https://app.example.com"}
    )
    assert response.headers.get("access-control-allow-origin") == "https://app.example.com"

async def test_cors_disallowed_origin(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.get(
        "/health",
        headers={"Origin": "https://evil.example.com"}
    )
    assert "access-control-allow-origin" not in response.headers

async def test_rate_limit_exceeded(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers):
    """Verify rate limiting kicks in after threshold."""
    # Make requests up to and beyond the limit
    for _ in range(100):
        await client.get("/api/v1/users/", headers=auth_headers)

    # Next request should be rate limited
    response = await client.get("/api/v1/users/", headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 429
    assert "retry-after" in response.headers

async def test_security_headers_present(client: AsyncClient):
    response = await client.get("/health")
    assert response.headers.get("x-content-type-options") == "nosniff"
    assert response.headers.get("x-frame-options") == "DENY"
    assert "strict-transport-security" in response.headers
generates realistic test data and handles relationships, reducing fixture boilerplate:

# tests/factories.py
import factory
import factory.fuzzy
from faker import Faker
from passlib.context import CryptContext
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from domains.users.models import User
from domains.orders.models import Order, OrderItem
from domains.products.models import Product

fake = Faker()
pwd_context = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto")

class AsyncSQLAlchemyOptions(factory.base.FactoryOptions):
    pass

class UserFactory(factory.Factory):
    class Meta:
        model = User

    username = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: fake.user_name()[:50])
    email = factory.LazyFunction(fake.email)
    hashed_password = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: pwd_context.hash("testpassword123"))
    is_active = True

    @classmethod
    async def create_async(cls, session: AsyncSession, **kwargs):
        user = cls.build(**kwargs)
        session.add(user)
        await session.flush()
        return user

class ProductFactory(factory.Factory):
    class Meta:
        model = Product

    sku = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: f"SKU-{fake.uuid4()[:8].upper()}")
    name = factory.LazyFunction(fake.catch_phrase)
    price = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyFloat(0.99, 999.99)
    stock = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyInteger(0, 1000)
    is_active = True

    @classmethod
    async def create_async(cls, session: AsyncSession, **kwargs):
        product = cls.build(**kwargs)
        session.add(product)
        await session.flush()
        return product

class OrderFactory(factory.Factory):
    class Meta:
        model = Order

    user_id = factory.SelfAttribute("user.id")
    total = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyFloat(10.0, 500.0)
    status = factory.fuzzy.FuzzyChoice(["pending", "confirmed", "shipped", "delivered"])

    @classmethod
    async def create_async(cls, session: AsyncSession, user: User = None, **kwargs):
        if user is None:
            user = await UserFactory.create_async(session)
        order = cls.build(user_id=user.id, **kwargs)
        session.add(order)
        await session.flush()
        return order

async def test_order_history(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers, db_session, existing_user):
    # Create 5 orders for the test user
    for _ in range(5):
        await OrderFactory.create_async(db_session, user=existing_user)

    response = await client.get("/api/v1/orders/", headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json()["total"] == 5

@pytest.mark.parametrize("method,path,expected_status", [
    ("GET",    "/api/v1/users/",     401),
    ("POST",   "/api/v1/users/",     401),
    ("GET",    "/api/v1/orders/",    401),
    ("POST",   "/api/v1/orders/",    401),
    ("GET",    "/api/v1/products/",  200),    # Public endpoint
    ("GET",    "/health",            200),    # Health check — no auth
])
async def test_endpoint_auth_requirements(client: AsyncClient, method: str, path: str, expected_status: int):
    response = await client.request(method, path)
    assert response.status_code == expected_status

@pytest.mark.parametrize("price,expected_status", [
    (0.01, 201),       # Minimum valid price
    (9999.99, 201),    # Large valid price
    (0, 422),          # Zero — invalid (gt=0)
    (-1.0, 422),       # Negative — invalid
    ("free", 422),     # Non-numeric
])
async def test_product_price_validation(client: AsyncClient, admin_headers, price, expected_status):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/products/", json={
        "name": "Test Product",
        "sku": "TEST-001",
        "price": price,
        "stock": 100
    }, headers=admin_headers)
    assert response.status_code == expected_status
A complete test module for an order creation endpoint that verifies the entire stack — validation, auth, database interaction, inventory management, and response structure:

# tests/integration/test_orders.py
import pytest
from httpx import AsyncClient
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from tests.factories import UserFactory, ProductFactory, OrderFactory

@pytest.fixture
async def product_in_stock(db_session: AsyncSession):
    return await ProductFactory.create_async(
        db_session,
        sku="WIDGET-001",
        price=29.99,
        stock=100
    )

@pytest.fixture
async def product_out_of_stock(db_session: AsyncSession):
    return await ProductFactory.create_async(
        db_session,
        sku="RARE-001",
        price=99.99,
        stock=0
    )

async def test_create_order_success(
    client: AsyncClient,
    auth_headers: dict,
    product_in_stock,
    db_session: AsyncSession
):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/orders/", json={
        "items": [{"product_id": product_in_stock.id, "quantity": 3}]
    }, headers=auth_headers)

    assert response.status_code == 201
    data = response.json()

    # Response structure
    assert "order_id" in data
    assert "reference" in data
    assert data["status"] == "pending"
    assert abs(data["total"] - (29.99 * 3)) < 0.01   # Float comparison

    # Items are included
    assert len(data["items"]) == 1
    assert data["items"][0]["quantity"] == 3
    assert abs(data["items"][0]["unit_price"] - 29.99) < 0.01

    # Verify stock was decremented in DB
    await db_session.refresh(product_in_stock)
    assert product_in_stock.stock == 97  # 100 - 3

async def test_create_order_insufficient_stock(
    client: AsyncClient,
    auth_headers: dict,
    product_out_of_stock
):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/orders/", json={
        "items": [{"product_id": product_out_of_stock.id, "quantity": 1}]
    }, headers=auth_headers)

    assert response.status_code == 409
    assert "stock" in response.json()["detail"].lower()

async def test_create_order_nonexistent_product(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/orders/", json={
        "items": [{"product_id": 99999, "quantity": 1}]
    }, headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 404

async def test_create_order_empty_items_rejected(client: AsyncClient, auth_headers: dict):
    response = await client.post("/api/v1/orders/", json={"items": []}, headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 422

async def test_order_only_visible_to_owner(
    client: AsyncClient,
    db_session: AsyncSession,
    existing_user,
    auth_headers: dict,
    test_settings
):
    # Create an order belonging to a different user
    other_user = await UserFactory.create_async(db_session)
    order = await OrderFactory.create_async(db_session, user=other_user)

    # existing_user tries to access other_user's order
    response = await client.get(
        f"/api/v1/orders/{order.id}",
        headers=auth_headers
    )
    assert response.status_code == 404   # 404, not 403 (doesn't reveal existence)

async def test_order_list_only_returns_own_orders(
    client: AsyncClient,
    db_session: AsyncSession,
    existing_user,
    auth_headers: dict
):
    # Create 3 orders for the test user and 5 for a different user
    for _ in range(3):
        await OrderFactory.create_async(db_session, user=existing_user)

    other_user = await UserFactory.create_async(db_session)
    for _ in range(5):
        await OrderFactory.create_async(db_session, user=other_user)

    response = await client.get("/api/v1/orders/", headers=auth_headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    # Only the 3 orders belonging to existing_user are returned
    assert data["total"] == 3

async def test_concurrent_order_stock_safety(
    client: AsyncClient,
    auth_headers: dict,
    db_session: AsyncSession
):
    """Verify that concurrent orders don't oversell inventory."""
    import asyncio

    product = await ProductFactory.create_async(db_session, stock=5)

    # Simulate 10 concurrent orders for 1 unit each (only 5 should succeed)
    tasks = [
        client.post("/api/v1/orders/", json={
            "items": [{"product_id": product.id, "quantity": 1}]
        }, headers=auth_headers)
        for _ in range(10)
    ]
    responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    status_codes = [r.status_code for r in responses if not isinstance(r, Exception)]
    successes = status_codes.count(201)
    conflicts = status_codes.count(409)

    # Exactly 5 should succeed, 5 should fail with 409
    assert successes == 5
    assert conflicts == 5

    # Verify stock is exactly 0
    await db_session.refresh(product)
    assert product.stock == 0
1. Not using transaction rollback between tests

        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.drop_all)
        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all)
    yield
Use transaction rollback instead — it is orders of magnitude faster.

Always clear overrides in fixture teardown (
Status codes are necessary but not sufficient. Verify response shape, field values, database state, and that sensitive fields are absent.

# WRONG — blocks the event loop
import time
time.sleep(1)

# CORRECT — yields to event loop
import asyncio
await asyncio.sleep(1)

Testing only happy paths leaves authorization bypasses, validation gaps, and edge cases undetected. For every endpoint, test: missing auth, wrong auth, wrong roles, invalid input shapes, boundary values, and resource-not-found cases.
Database-backed fixtures should be function-scoped unless the underlying transaction rollback guarantees isolation.
Use transaction rollback, not table truncation
— isolate tests via rolled-back transactions for speed and reliability.
— it exercises routing, middleware, validation, serialization, and response models together.
Always test that sensitive fields are absent from responses

— unauthenticated, wrong token, expired token, insufficient roles, and cross-user data access.
for field validation, enum values, and edge cases to achieve high coverage with minimal test code.
for test data creation — it reduces fixture boilerplate and makes relationships explicit.
Never share the production database URL with tests
— validate test configuration explicitly at the start of the test session.
— a leaked override can silently affect every subsequent test in the session.
Assert database state, not just HTTP responses
— verify that inventory was decremented, records were created, and side effects occurred, using the
where fixtures support it — each worker gets its own transaction scope, reducing total test runtime significantly for large suites.
is the primary mechanism for test isolation — it replaces any
callable with a test-controlled alternative without modifying production code.
is the correct async testing client — it exercises the full ASGI stack including middleware, routing, and serialization without a network socket.
The transaction rollback fixture pattern — each test runs inside a transaction that is rolled back — provides both complete isolation and significantly faster execution than schema recreation.
Unit tests of service classes with mocked repositories are faster and more focused than integration tests, and should cover all business rule branches including error cases.
Testing should verify absence of sensitive fields (
filtering is easily broken by refactoring and must be tested to be trusted.
Every protected endpoint needs at minimum four auth tests: no token, invalid token, expired token, and insufficient permissions — these are the most common security regressions.
is the correct tool for validation boundary testing — it produces readable, comprehensive coverage of field constraints with minimal duplication.
are the only way to detect race conditions in stock management, idempotency, and shared resource handling — they must be part of the test suite for any resource with contention semantics.
